# Code Quality Automation

This document lists the automated checks Lean Pool runs in addition to the standard mathlib linter set (in-Lean `#lint`, text-style linters, `shake`, doc-gen4, axiom audit, etc.). Each item is a CI job we own; mathlib-equivalent jobs are inherited where possible.

## 1. Proof performance hotspots

**What.** On every PR, profile the 10 slowest theorems in changed files (and the 10 slowest in the library overall) using `lean_profile_proof`. Post a comment summarizing tactic-level hotspots and any regressions versus `main`.

**Why.** Mathlib catches build-time bloat at the file level via `bench`; we want finer granularity so contributors can see which tactic in their proof is expensive before review.

**Enforcement.**

- Soft fail (warning comment) by default.
- Hard fail if any single proof's elapsed time regresses by more than X% versus `main`, without a justification comment.
- Persist per-theorem timings to a `bench/` artifact so trends are visible across PRs.

(Heartbeat budgets themselves are enforced separately — see §6.)

## 2. Folder-level docs as Lean files

**What.** Every directory under `LeanPool/` contains an `Index.lean` (or named after the folder) whose only content is `import`s of the folder's files plus a top-of-file module docstring describing scope, contents, and intended audience.

**Why.** doc-gen4 renders module docstrings on the generated docs site; a plain `README.md` does not. This mirrors mathlib's pattern (e.g. `Mathlib/Algebra.lean`).

**Enforcement.**

- CI checks every directory containing `.lean` files has a corresponding index file.
- The index file must start with a `/-! ... -/` module docstring of at least N characters (small floor, just to prevent empties).
- The root `LeanPool.lean` is regenerated by script and diff-checked, the same way mathlib does `mk_all`.

## 3. Provenance metadata

Lean Pool positions itself as arXiv-for-formal-math, so provenance is a first-class artifact, not a comment convention.

### 3a. File header

Every `.lean` file begins with a structured copyright/provenance header:

```lean
/-
Copyright (c) 2026 <authors>. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: <names>

Source: arxiv:2401.12345          -- optional, one of arxiv/doi/url
MSC: 11M06, 11N05                 -- optional, comma-separated
Tags: analytic-number-theory      -- domain tags, controlled vocabulary
-/
```

A linter parses the header and fails CI on missing required fields (copyright, license, authors) and malformed optional fields.

### 3b. Per-theorem provenance attribute

A `@[provenance ...]` attribute lets contributors point individual results at their informal source:

```lean
@[provenance arxiv:2401.12345 §3.2 Thm 1]
theorem main_result : ... := by ...
```

The attribute is a no-op at elaboration time but is read by the docs generator and indexing pipeline.

### 3c. Auto-generated domain index

A CI job groups every theorem by its `Tags:` and emits a browsable index page (rendered alongside doc-gen4 output). Contributors don't maintain this by hand.

### 3d. Verification badge

For each file, CI emits one of:

- **verified** — no `sorry`, no new axioms outside the allowlist (`Classical.choice`, `propext`, `Quot.sound`).
- **draft** — contains `sorry` or transitively depends on a file that does.
- **extra-axiom** — uses an axiom outside the allowlist (must be declared in the file header).

The badge is shown on the docs site next to the file and on the auto-generated index.

## 4. Semantic dedup (incl. compare-to-mathlib)

**What.** On every PR, extract the statements of newly added or modified `theorem`s and run them through `lean-explore` / `leansearch`. Post a comment with the top-k candidate matches across:

1. mathlib (catches "this should be upstreamed instead"),
2. existing Lean Pool content (catches reproving),
3. other indexed Lean packages.

**Why.** A faster review pipeline only works if it doesn't accumulate near-duplicates. The compare-to-mathlib check is folded in here — `lean-explore` indexes mathlib, so the same job covers both directions.

**Enforcement.**

- Always advisory (a comment, never a block) — the author decides whether the match is real.
- For matches above a similarity threshold, the comment additionally suggests either deprecating the local copy in favor of the mathlib name, or opening a mathlib PR.

## 5. Size limits

**What.** Two hard caps:

- **No `.lean` file may exceed 2000 lines.**
- **No single proof (the term following `:=` for a `theorem`/`lemma`, or the tactic block) may exceed 100 lines.**

**Why.** Files and proofs that grow past these thresholds are almost always doing too much; splitting them up is the cheapest correctness and review aid we have.

**Enforcement.**

- Text linter for the file cap (count non-blank, non-comment lines; fail CI on overflow).
- AST-aware linter for the proof cap, using `lean_file_outline` ranges to measure each declaration's body. (A regex over `theorem ... := by ... <next decl>` is the v0; an `Elab`-based linter is v1.)
- Either limit can be waived per-declaration with an inline `-- size-limit-ok: <reason>` comment, which the linter requires to be non-empty.

## 6. Default heartbeats

**What.** No file may raise heartbeat limits. The following are forbidden anywhere in the repository:

- `set_option maxHeartbeats <n>` for any `n` other than the default
- `set_option synthInstance.maxHeartbeats <n>` (likewise)
- `maxHeartbeats` / `synthInstance.maxHeartbeats` passed via `lakefile.toml` `moreLeanArgs`

**Why.** Bumping heartbeats hides slow proofs instead of fixing them. If a proof needs more heartbeats, the proof is the problem — refactor it, split a lemma out, replace `simp` with `simp only [...]`, or precompute terms. Keeping the limit at default makes performance regressions surface as build failures rather than silent slowdowns.

**Enforcement.**

- Text linter (regex over `.lean` files): fail CI on any `maxHeartbeats` or `synthInstance.maxHeartbeats` override.
- Same regex applied to `lakefile.toml` and `lakefile.lean`.
- No waiver mechanism. If a proof genuinely cannot be brought under default heartbeats, that's a discussion for the PR, not a quiet `set_option`.

## 7. Stale-bot

**What.** Standard `actions/stale` configuration:

- PRs idle > 30 days → labeled `stale`, comment posted.
- Idle a further 14 days → closed.
- Issues idle > 90 days → labeled `stale`; closed after 30 more.
- `pinned`, `roadmap`, `good-first-issue` labels exempt.

**Why.** Throughput is the project's reason for existing. Stale PRs/issues either get unblocked or move out of the queue.

**Enforcement.** `.github/workflows/stale.yml` running daily.

## Implementation order

1. Size limits (cheapest, highest signal-to-noise).
2. Default heartbeats (one regex; sets the performance bar before anything grows).
3. File header + verification badge — establishes the provenance schema everything else builds on.
4. Folder-level Lean docs + root-import regeneration.
5. Stale-bot (one config file).
6. Semantic dedup (depends on a stable `lean-explore` integration; advisory only at first).
7. Per-theorem provenance attribute + domain index page.
8. Proof performance hotspots (most expensive to wire up; do last).
